text:
When setting project goals, it is helpful to use the SMART framework. This means your goals should be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. For example, instead of saying 'improve sales,' a SMART goal would be 'increase sales by 10% within three months.'

To track progress, break the goal into milestones. These are smaller checkpoints, like completing market research by the first month. Also, define the main deliverables, such as a final report or a new product design. Finally, ensure stakeholder alignment—everyone involved should agree on the goals before starting. This keeps the team focused and reduces misunderstandings throughout the project.
